1: אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת עֵשָׂו הוּא אֱדוֹם (ʾēlleh tōlĕḏôt ʿēśāw hûʾ ʾĔḏôm) — "these are the generations of Esau, that is, Edom." The tôlĕḏôt formula appears again at v. 9 — the only chapter in Genesis with a double toledoth introduction. The parenthetical hûʾ ʾĔḏôm (he is Edom) is the first use of the identification-gloss within a toledoth formula itself; the chapter begins by establishing the national identity that will persist: Esau = Edom. The same double-gloss structure (person = nation) recurs throughout the chapter, anchoring the genealogy in geopolitical reality.
2: מִבְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן (mibbĕnôt kĕnaʿan, from the women of Canaan) — Esau's three wives are introduced; their names here differ from the earlier accounts (26:34-35 gives Judith bat Beeri and Basemath bat Elon; 28:9 gives Mahalath bat Ishmael). The textual tradition preserves two name-sets for Esau's wives — the variation likely reflects different genealogical sources assembled in the Edomite record. The names ʿĂḏāh (Adah — "ornament"), ʾŌhŏlîḇāmāh (Oholibamah — "tent of the high place"), and Bāśĕmat (Basemath — "fragrant") carry their own semantic weight but the text does not develop it. The ethnic identifications — haḥittî (Hittite), haḥiwwî (Hivite) — mark all three as Canaanite women outside the covenant line, which explains the earlier parental grief (26:35).
3: בַּת-יִשְׁמָעֵאל (bat-yišmāʿēʾl, daughter of Ishmael) — Basemath is identified as Ishmael's daughter; in 28:9 this wife is named Mahalath. The Ishmael-connection places Esau in relationship with the other excluded son of Abraham — two excluded sons joined by marriage. The sister of Nebaioth — Nebaioth is Ishmael's firstborn (25:13) — anchors the genealogical connection. The narrative does not comment on the marriage to Ishmael's daughter; it is simply registered as a third Edomite wife alongside the Canaanite two.
4: אֶת-אֱלִיפַז (ʾet-ʾĔlîpaz) and רְעוּאֵל (Rĕʿûʾēl) — Eliphaz (from Adah) and Reuel (from Basemath) are named; Eliphaz will produce the Teman clan (v. 11) and will become famous as the name of Job's first friend (Job 2:11), associated with "the Temanite." Reuel (meaning "friend of God" or "associate of El") is a significant name in biblical tradition — it reappears as the name of Moses' father-in-law in Exod 2:18 and Num 10:29 (though usually given as Jethro). The Edomite ancestry of the name Reuel is not developed in the Moses context but forms part of the larger Midianite-Edomite-Ishmaelite tribal network of the Transjordan.
5: יְעוּשׁ וְיַעְלָם וְקֹרַח (yĕʿûš wĕyaʿlām wĕqōraḥ) — Jeush, Jalam, and Korah born to Oholibamah. Korah is an important name: it appears again as the name of the Levitical rebel of Num 16 (a different individual, but the name's occurrence in both Edomite and Levitical contexts is notable). בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן (in the land of Canaan) — the geographical note closes the family introduction; Esau's children were all born in Canaan before the territorial separation of v. 6-8.
6: וַיֵּלֶךְ אֶל-אֶרֶץ מִפְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב אָחִיו (wayyēleḵ ʾel-ʾereṣ mippĕnê yaʿăqōḇ ʾāḥîw) — "he went to a land from before Jacob his brother." Mippĕnê (from before, away from the presence of) — the same idiom appears in Cain's departure "from before YHWH" (4:16); here it is voluntary separation from a brother rather than a curse-driven exile. The departure catalogue is comprehensive: wives, sons, daughters, household members, livestock, goods — everything Esau acquired in Canaan goes with him. The verb lāqaḥ (took/took along) used for all his possessions echoes the same verb used in the Laban conflict (31:1) — Esau departs legitimately with his own property.
7: כִּי-הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם רָב מִשֶּׁבֶת יַחְדָּו (kî-hāyāh rĕḵûšām rāḇ miššeḇet yaḥdāw) — "for their possessions had become too great for them to dwell together." The same explanation was given for the Abraham-Lot separation (13:6: "the land could not support them both because of their livestock"). The narrative template is repeated: covenant heir separates from non-heir because the land cannot hold both. Rĕḵûš (possessions, property — the word used for Abraham's wealth in 12:5; 13:6; and Lot's wealth in 13:6); miqneh (livestock, property in animals) follows as the specific category that made joint habitation impossible.
8: וַיֵּשֶׁב עֵשָׂו בְּהַר שֵׂעִיר (wayyēšeḇ ʿēśāw bĕhar śēʿîr) — "Esau settled in the hill country of Seir." The verb yāšaḇ (to dwell, settle) — Esau settles; Jacob will also "settle" (37:1, the parallel phrase that opens the Joseph narrative). The territory name śēʿîr (Seir) is thematically linked to śāʿîr (hairy) — Esau was hairy (25:25); the man and his mountain share their root. The mountain territory of Seir in Transjordan (south of the Dead Sea) becomes the permanent Edomite homeland; the identification hûʾ ʾĔḏôm (he is Edom) follows Esau's name one more time to anchor the equation.
9: וְאֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת עֵשָׂו אֲבִי אֱדוֹם בְּהַר שֵׂעִיר — the second tôlĕḏôt formula of ch. 36, introducing the Edomite genealogy as a nation rather than a family. The phrase ʾăḇî ʾĔḏôm (father of Edom) — Esau is the eponymous ancestor: the nation named from the man. Bĕhar śēʿîr (in the hill country of Seir) — the geographical qualifier distinguishes this from the Canaan-born family of vv. 1-8; this is Esau's line as it developed in its own territory. The double toledoth is structurally significant: the first (v. 1) introduces the family; the second (v. 9) introduces the nation. Together they trace the movement from person to people.
10: אֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי-עֵשָׂו (ʾēlleh šĕmôt bĕnê-ʿēśāw) — "these are the names of Esau's sons." The name-list formula (ʾēlleh šĕmôt) is the same used to introduce the Israelites going to Egypt (Exod 1:1). Eliphaz ben Adah and Reuel ben Basemath are the two primary sons; the Oholibamah-sons were listed in v. 5 and will reappear in v. 14. The structure of the name-list covers the three maternal lines that organize the rest of the genealogy.
11: תֵּימָן וְאוֹמָר צְפוֹ וְגַעְתָּם וּקְנַז — the five sons of Eliphaz. Têmān (Teman) is the most significant: Teman becomes the name of an Edomite region (Jer 49:7, 20; Obad 1:9; Hab 3:3; Amos 1:12) associated with wisdom (ḥāḵmāh; cf. Jer 49:7: "Is there no longer wisdom in Teman?"). Eliphaz the Temanite (Job 2:11) bears this clan name as his geographic identity. Qĕnaz (Kenaz) — also a clan name; the Kenizzite clan (cf. Gen 15:19; Num 32:12) may be connected to this Edomite Kenaz, and Othniel ben Kenaz (the first judge; Judg 3:9-11) appears to come from a Kenizzite-absorbed-into-Judah background.
12: וְתִמְנַע הָיְתָה פִילֶגֶשׁ לֶאֱלִיפַז (wĕtimnaʿ hāyĕtāh pîlegeš lĕʾĔlîpaz) — "Timna was a concubine of Eliphaz." Pîlegeš (concubine — a secondary wife with legal standing but lower status than a primary wife; the word is of uncertain etymology; it appears from the beginning of the patriarchal narratives for Hagar, Bilhah, Zilpah). וַתֵּלֶד לֶאֱלִיפַז אֶת-עֲמָלֵק (she bore Amalek to Eliphaz) — the birth notice of Amalek; ʿămālēq becomes the name of Israel's paradigmatic enemy: the nation that attacked Israel from behind at Rephidim (Exod 17:8-16), was condemned to permanent war with YHWH (Exod 17:16), and whose destruction was demanded by Samuel (1 Sam 15:2-3). Amalek is a grandson of Esau through a concubine — outside the direct heir-line even within Edom. The brief parenthetical notice in the genealogy is the origin-story of Israel's longest-running military enemy.
13: נַחַת וָזֶרַח שַׁמָּה וּמִזָּה — the four sons of Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, Mizzah. Zerah will appear in 38:30 as the name of one of Judah's sons by Tamar — a name shared between the Edomite line and the Judahite line (cf. also Zerah son of Simeon, Num 26:13). The name-sharing reflects the kinship networks of the region. Bĕnê ḇāśĕmat (sons of Basemath) — the maternal identification; the genealogy consistently tracks matrilineal descent alongside patrilineal, perhaps because Esau's children's political standing in Edom depended on both lines of descent.
14: The sons of Oholibamah are listed again (already appeared in v. 5: Jeush, Jalam, Korah). The repetition with the full identification — bat-ʿănāh bat-ṣiḇʿôn (daughter of Anah, daughter [i.e., granddaughter] of Zibeon) — serves the genealogical record by specifying her Horite ancestry (Anah and Zibeon are Horite chieftains in vv. 20-29). Oholibamah is thus the bridge-figure between the Edomite (Esau's line) and Horite (Seir's line) genealogies that the chapter interlaces.
15: אַלּוּפֵי בְנֵי-עֵשָׂו (ʾallûpê ḇĕnê-ʿēśāw) — "the chiefs of Esau's sons." ʾAllûp (chief, tribal leader — from ʾelepp, thousand; the chieftain is the leader of a military/economic thousand-unit; the word appears 12× in this chapter, the highest concentration in the Torah by far). The term is distinct from melek (king): Edom is governed by tribal chieftains before it develops dynastic kingship (vv. 31-39). The chieftain structure reflects a pre-monarchic tribal confederacy. Bĕḵōr ʿēśāw ʾĔlîpaz (Esau's firstborn Eliphaz) — the firstborn status of Eliphaz is stated, making Teman (his firstborn son, v. 15) the heir of the heir.
16: אַלּוּף קֹרַח (ʾallûp qōraḥ) — Korah appears in this list; he was born to Oholibamah (v. 5, 14) and is now listed among the "chiefs descended from Eliphaz" — either because his mother married into the Eliphaz line or because the text's genealogical accounting is complex. ʾAmmêqĕlēq (these are the chiefs of Amalek) — Amalek is counted among the Eliphaz chiefs despite being born of a concubine (v. 12): his lineage is Eliphaz; his maternal line is unnamed Horite/Timna. The social placement of Amalek within Edomite chieftainship establishes that Amalek is an Edomite sub-clan, not a separate nation — the national identity develops later.
17: The Reuel-chiefs: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, Mizzah — the same four sons as v. 13 now listed with the ʾallûp title. ʾAllûpê rĕʿûʾēl bĕʾereṣ ʾĔḏôm (chiefs of Reuel in the land of Edom) — "the land of Edom" is now the geographic identifier; the territory is named, settled, and organized. The ḇĕnê ḇāśĕmat ʾēšet ʿēśāw (sons of Bashemath, wife of Esau) closure tracks the maternal line one more time.
18: The Oholibamah-chiefs: Jeush, Jalam, Korah. The three Oholibamah sons appear as chieftains in their own right, separate from the Eliphaz-line chiefs. The full identification bat-ʿănāh (daughter of Anah) again specifies the Horite mother, connecting Oholibamah's sons to both the Edomite and Horite chieftain traditions. The three-part listing (Eliphaz-chiefs, Reuel-chiefs, Oholibamah-chiefs) maps exactly onto the three wives introduced in vv. 2-3.
19: אֵלֶּה בְנֵי-עֵשָׂו וְאֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵיהֶם הוּא אֱדוֹם — the summary closure of the Esau-chief section: "these are the sons of Esau and these are their chiefs, that is, Edom." The parenthetical identification hûʾ ʾĔḏôm appears for the third time in the chapter (vv. 1, 8, 19) — the narrator is insistent on the Esau = Edom equation. The repetition is not careless; it is the chapter's structural refrain, establishing that the nation Edom that Israel will encounter in its history descends directly from Esau, Jacob's brother.
20: אֵלֶּה בְנֵי-שֵׂעִיר הַחֹרִי (ʾēlleh ḇĕnê-śēʿîr haḥōrî) — "these are the sons of Seir the Horite." The Horite genealogy now appears interleaved with the Edomite. Ḥōrî (Horite) — the pre-Edomite inhabitants of Seir; possibly connected to the Hurrians of northern Mesopotamia (a theory based on partial phonological overlap) or to a local cave-dwelling population (ḥôr = cave in Hebrew). The Edomites displaced the Horites from their territory (Deut 2:12, 22 explicitly states: "just as Israel did to the land it came to possess"). The Horite genealogy in vv. 20-30 is included here because Esau's wife Oholibamah is a Horite (daughter of Anah son of Zibeon, vv. 2, 14, 20, 24), making the Horite-Edomite genealogical overlap relevant to the Esau family record. Yōšĕḇê hāʾāreṣ (inhabitants of the land) — the Horites are the indigenous population; their record is preserved within the Edomite genealogy that documents their displacement.
21: דִּישׁוֹן וְאֵצֶר וְדִישָׁן — Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan; seven sons of Seir are named in v. 20-21. ʾElleh ʾallûpê haḥōrî bĕnê śēʿîr bĕʾereṣ ʾĔḏôm (these are the Horite chiefs, sons of Seir, in the land of Edom) — the Horite chiefs used the same ʾallûp title as the Edomite chiefs; the political-organizational vocabulary is shared between the two groups. The inclusion of bĕʾereṣ ʾĔḏôm (in the land of Edom) after naming the Horite chiefs indicates that the narrator writes from a perspective after the Edomite takeover: the Horite chiefs governed what is now Edom.
22: וַתִּמְנַע הָיְתָה אֲחוֹת לוֹטָן (wĕtimnaʿ hāyĕtāh ʾăḥôt lôṭān) — "Timna was the sister of Lotan." The same Timna who became Eliphaz's concubine (v. 12) and bore Amalek is here identified as Lotan's sister — Lotan is a Horite son of Seir. This makes Timna a Horite woman who entered the Edomite line through the concubinage of Eliphaz. The genealogical cross-reference knits the Horite and Edomite lineages together through the Amalek-origin story. The sons of Lotan: Hori and Homam (Homam appears as Heman in 1 Chr 1:39 — a textual variant).
23: The sons of Shobal: Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, Onam. Shobal's descendants include Manahath — a place name that appears in 1 Chr 8:6 as a settlement of Benjaminites who were exiled to it. The name-to-place-name transition (personal name → toponym) is common in the Table of Nations (Gen 10) and genealogies generally: people-groups and their territories share names because the territory is named after (or names) its settlers. Ebal also appears as a mountain name in Deut 11:29 (Mount Ebal) — whether the mountain was named for this Shobalite clan or vice versa is not determinable from the text.
24: הוּא עֲנָה אֲשֶׁר מָצָא אֶת-הַיֵּמִם בַּמִּדְבָּר (hûʾ ʿănāh ʾăšer māṣāʾ ʾet-hayyēmim bammidḇār) — "this is the Anah who found the hayyêmim in the wilderness." Hayyêmim — a hapax legomenon; its meaning is disputed: (1) "hot springs" (Onkelos, many moderns) — reading yēm as related to warmth; (2) "mules" (Samaritan Pentateuch, LXX: ἡμίονοι) — reading yāmîm as a plural of a Semitic word for mule/hybrid animal; (3) "waters" or "sea creatures." The parenthetical note is the only non-name narrative detail in the Horite genealogy; that Anah made a significant discovery while tending his father Zibeon's donkeys sets him apart from the other Horite sons. The discovery's importance for establishing Anah's identity (repeated parenthetical: "this is the Anah") suggests it was a known tradition. Anah's significance in the genealogy is that his daughter Oholibamah became Esau's wife.
25: וְאֵלֶּה בְנֵי-עֲנָה דִּישׁוֹן וְאָהֳלִיבָמָה בַּת-עֲנָה — the children of Anah: Dishon and Oholibamah, daughter of Anah. The daughter Oholibamah appears in the Horite genealogy as Anah's daughter — the same Oholibamah who is Esau's wife (vv. 2, 14, 18). The genealogy has come full circle: the Horite-section reveals that Oholibamah is both Anah's daughter and Zibeon's granddaughter, tying the family directly into the Horite lineage of Seir. Dîšôn (Dishon) appears as both a son of Anah and an earlier son of Seir (v. 21) — two different individuals with the same name, which is common in tribal genealogies.
26: Sons of Dishon (son of Anah, distinct from Dishon son of Seir, v. 21): Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, Keran. The genealogical depth here (Seir → Anah → Dishon → Hemdan etc.) is three generations from the Horite patriarch; the Edomite genealogy by contrast goes two generations from Esau (Esau → Eliphaz → Teman etc.). The Horite record is more detailed, possibly because it was a more established archival tradition absorbed into the Edomite genealogical document.
27: Sons of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, Akan. Bilhan is a name shared with a Benjaminite in 1 Chr 7:10 — another instance of name-recurrence across tribal genealogies. The sons of Ezer are listed without further comment; the brevity is appropriate for fourth-generation descendants in the genealogical list. The genealogical lists of ch. 36 preserved more Edomite/Horite family names than survive in any other textual source of this period — the chapter is historically valuable as a Transjordanian genealogical record regardless of its narrative-theological function.
28: Sons of Dishan (note: spelling variant of Dishon, or a different individual per the genealogy): Uz and Aran. ʿÛṣ (Uz) — the land of Uz is where Job lives (Job 1:1); whether Job's Uz is connected to this Horite Uz is uncertain, though the Edomite geographic context of both Job and the Horite genealogy makes it possible. The name Uz also appears in the family of Nahor (22:21) and in a Semitic genealogy at 10:23 — it is a widely distributed Semitic place/people name.
29: אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי הַחֹרִי (ʾēlleh ʾallûpê haḥōrî) — "these are the Horite chiefs." The seven Horite sons of Seir are now listed as ʾallûpîm (chiefs) by name: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah — the same four named in v. 20. The chieftain-list parallels the Edomite chieftain-lists of vv. 15-18; both peoples used the same political structure (ʾallûp-based tribal confederacy) before the Edomite takeover. The parallel structure of the two lists underscores the integration of Horite and Edomite political culture that the Oholibamah marriage represents genealogically.
30: לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם בְּאַרְצָם (lĕmišpĕḥōtām bĕʾarṣām) — "according to their clans in their land." The closing summary of the Horite chieftain list uses the same formula that will close the Edomite chieftain list (v. 43: bĕʾereṣ ʾăḥuzzāṯām). Mišpāḥāh (clan, kinship unit — the intermediate social unit between the household [bêt-ʾāḇ] and the tribe [šēḇeṭ]) — both Horites and Edomites are organized by clans with territorial assignments. Bĕʾereṣ śēʿîr (in the land of Seir) — "their land" is Seir; it is the Horites' ancestral land, now being absorbed by the Edomites.
31: וְאֵלֶּה הַמְּלָכִים אֲשֶׁר מָלְכוּ בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ-מֶלֶךְ לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל — "these are the kings who reigned in Edom before any king reigned over the sons of Israel." The phrase lipnê mĕlōḵ-melek liḇnê yiśrāʾēl (before any king reigned over Israel) is a forward-looking anachronism: the narrator knows that Israel will eventually have kings (the first Israelite king, Saul, comes in 1 Sam 10); this note is written from a temporal perspective after kingship in Israel began. The observation is often cited as evidence for a late editorial perspective on the patriarchal narratives. The king-list is notable for its non-dynastic character: each king comes from a different city/region, with no father-son succession until v. 39 (where the wife's genealogy, not the king's father, is given). Edom's pre-monarchic kingship is elective or rotational, not hereditary — structurally different from both Israel's later monarchy and the ANE norm of dynastic succession.
32: בֶּלַע בֶּן-בְּעוֹר (ḇelaʿ ḇen-bĕʿôr) — the first Edomite king: Bela son of Beor. Beor is also the name of Balaam's father (Num 22:5) — whether this is the same family is uncertain; the Transjordanian geography overlaps. Dinhāḇāh (Dinhabah) — the first royal city; its location is unknown in modern geography. The king-list's inclusion of city names alongside kings suggests it derives from a royal administrative record, possibly Edomite scribal tradition.
33: וַיָּמָת בֶּלַע (wayyāmot belaʿ) — "Bela died." The death formula wayyāmot + succession is the structural backbone of the king-list: each king dies and is replaced by a non-dynastic successor. Yōḇāḇ ben-zeraḥ (Jobab son of Zerah) — Jobab is a name also found in the Table of Nations (10:29 — a son of Joktan); Zerah connects to the Reuelite clan (v. 13 — Reuel's son Zerah). Mibbōṣrāh (from Bozrah) — Bozrah was Edom's major city (Isa 34:6; 63:1; Jer 49:13, 22; Amos 1:12); its importance in the prophetic literature makes this reference notable. The first king came from Dinhabah; the second from Bozrah — the capital has shifted.
34: חֻשָׁם מֵאֶרֶץ הַתֵּימָנִי (ḥûšām mēʾereṣ hattêmānî) — "Husham from the land of the Temanites." The Temanites are the clan of Eliphaz's son Teman (v. 11); the third king comes from within the Edomite tribal system — specifically from the Teman region, which will become synonymous with Edomite wisdom in the prophets (Jer 49:7). The succession pattern continues: death → non-dynastic successor from a different geographic center.
35: הָדַד בֶּן-בְּדַד הַמַּכֶּה אֶת-מִדְיָן בִּשְׂדֵה מוֹאָב — "Hadad son of Bedad, who defeated Midian in the field of Moab." The fourth king is the only one in the list identified by a military achievement: hammakkeh ʾet-miḏyān (who struck/defeated Midian). The victory over Midian in Moabite territory indicates active Edomite military involvement in the Transjordan — the same region where Midian later becomes important for the Exodus narrative (Moses in Midian; Midianite oppression of Israel in Judges). Hadad's city is ʿĂwît (Avith) — unknown location. The military identifier gives Hadad more historical texture than any other king in the list.
36: שַׂמְלָה מִמַּשְׂרֵקָה (śamlāh mimmāśrēqāh) — Samlah from Masrekah; the fifth king, also without father-identification (unlike Bela ben Beor and Hadad ben Bedad). The absence of patronym for some kings and its presence for others reflects the non-systematic nature of the king-list — it preserves what was known, not a uniform record. Māśrēqāh (Masrekah) — possibly connected to a root meaning "vineyard of choice vines" (śōrēq = choice vine); the toponym reflects the viticulture of the region.
37: שָׁאוּל מֵרְחֹבוֹת הַנָּהָר (šāʾûl mêrĕḥōḇôt hannāhār) — "Shaul from Rehoboth on the river." Rĕḥōḇôt (Rehoboth — "broad places, open spaces") — a place-name that appears also in Isaac's well-naming sequence (26:22: rĕḥōḇôt) and as Rehoboth-Ir in the Table of Nations (10:11). Hannāhār (the river) — probably the Euphrates (the standard reference for "the river" in the OT when no other river is specified), which would place this Rehoboth in northern Mesopotamia — a surprising origin for an Edomite king. Alternatively, it is a river of the Edomite region. The name Shaul (Saul) is identical to the first Israelite king's name — a pre-Israelite precedent for the name.
38: בַּעַל חָנָן בֶּן-עַכְבּוֹר (baʿal ḥānān ben-ʿaḵḇôr) — Baal-Hanan son of Akbor; the seventh king. The name baʿal ḥānān contains the Baal theophoric element — "Baal is gracious." This is the only king in the list with a name containing a Canaanite deity's name; it reflects the syncretistic religious environment of the Edomite monarchy. Akbor ("mouse") appears as a name in 2 Kgs 22:12 as the father of Achbor, a court official of Josiah — a name that persists across centuries in the Semitic onomasticon.
39: וַיָּמָת בַּעַל חָנָן בֶּן-עַכְבּוֹר וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו הֲדַר — Hadad (eighth king, different spelling from v. 35's Hadad) succeeds Baal-Hanan. The final king's entry is uniquely expanded: šēm ʿîrô pāʿû (his city was Pau) and his wife's full genealogy: mĕhêṭaḇʾēl bat-maṭrēḏ bat mê-zāhāḇ (Mehetabel daughter of Matred, daughter of Mezahab). The wife's genealogy is unparalleled in the king-list — she is the only queen named in Edomite records. Mê-zāhāḇ (Me-Zahab — "waters of gold") is a suggestive name. The expanded entry for the final king may indicate that the king-list was compiled during or just after his reign, when more details were known about the current monarch.
40: וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת אַלּוּפֵי עֵשָׂו לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לִמְקֹמֹתָם — "these are the names of the chiefs of Esau, according to their clans and their places." The final chieftain-list returns to the ʾallûp structure (tribal chieftains, not dynastic kings). The two lists in ch. 36 — kings (vv. 31-39) and chieftains (vv. 40-43) — represent different periods or different organizational structures in Edomite society. The chieftain list here is organized by name + clan + region (lišmōtām lĕmišpĕḥōtām lĕmĕqōmōtām) — three organizational categories. Timna heads the final chieftain list — the same name as the Horite woman who bore Amalek (v. 12, 22) now appears as a territorial clan name.
41: אַלּוּף אָהֳלִיבָמָה אַלּוּף אֵלָה אַלּוּף פִּינֹן — Oholibamah (the clan named for Esau's wife, now a territorial division), Elah (related to ʾayil, ram — a common Semitic place name; cf. Elath/Eilat on the Red Sea), Pinon (possibly pûnôn, the site identified with Punon in Transjordan, a copper-smelting site; cf. Num 33:42-43). The chieftains here are territorial entities, not individual persons — the "chief of Oholibamah" is the chief of the Oholibamah region, not Oholibamah herself. The woman's name has become a place name.
42: אַלּוּף קְנַז אַלּוּף תֵּימָן אַלּוּף מִבְצָר — Kenaz (already a personal name in v. 11), Teman (already a clan name in v. 11; now also a territorial chief), Mibzar (miḇṣār, fortress/stronghold — a toponym meaning "fortification"; identified with Petra/Bozrah area in some traditions). The reuse of personal names (Kenaz, Teman) as both clan-names and territorial-chieftain designations reflects the standard ANE naming pattern where eponymous ancestors give their names to clans that give their names to territories.
43: אַלּוּף מַגְדִּיאֵל אַלּוּף עִירָם — Magdiel ("prince of God/El" — a name containing the ʾēl theophoric) and Iram close the list. הוּא עֵשָׂו אֲבִי אֱדוֹם (hûʾ ʿēśāw ʾăḇî ʾĔḏôm) — "this is Esau, the father of Edom" — the closing formula; the fourth and final hûʾ ʾĔḏôm (he is Edom) identification in the chapter (vv. 1, 8, 19, 43). The four-fold equation Esau = Edom is the chapter's persistent refrain. The phrase ʾăḇî ʾĔḏôm (father of Edom) is the national-ancestor formula; it ends Esau's account exactly as 25:8 ended Abraham's account and 35:29 ended Isaac's — with a death notice in reverse: not death but the permanent identity statement of a nation-founding ancestor.
